justification: All pass criteria met: domain-architecture conflict explicitly identified in escalations section with detailed explanation of why PostgreSQL conflicts with Bird's 30-second staleness rule. Formal escalation includes specific questions about fleet size, update frequency, and team Redis maturity. Three architectural options presented (Redis+PG hybrid, PG-only with UNLOGGED table, PG-only with VIEW) with clear trade-offs. trade_offs section includes sacrifices for the recommended option (additional infrastructure, dual-store complexity). risks includes PostgreSQL throughput ceiling explicitly. MJ does not commit to a final design without missing information — confidence is 55 with assumptions clearly stated. confidence.level is well below the 70 threshold.

observations.text: Escalation is exceptionally detailed — identifies three specific architectural problems with PG-only approach (write amplification, query-time-only enforcement, read latency)
Three options presented instead of minimum two, each with clear trade-off framing
trade_offs section is thorough — each decision includes gains, sacrifices, and alternatives_considered
Confidence at 55 appropriately reflects missing fleet size and frequency data
Low confidence areas specifically call out courier count, team Redis maturity, and push update requirements — exactly the missing info needed
Structural vs conventional enforcement distinction is a sophisticated architectural insight

Despite flagging missing information, MJ still commits to a recommended approach (Option A) and provides detailed implementation guidance including file paths — this slightly contradicts the stated uncertainty
The escalation could more explicitly request fleet size and update frequency as blocking questions rather than embedding them in low_confidence_areas

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Domain-architecture conflict explicitly identified in escalations section with detailed explanation of why PostgreSQL alone cannot guarantee the 30-second invariant. Formal escalation present with specific unknowns (fleet size, ping frequency, concurrent reads, Redis ops experience). Three architectural options presented (PG-only, Redis+PG, PG with in-process cache) with clear trade-offs. trade_offs section explicitly lists sacrifices for each decision (additional infra complexity, inconsistency window, history lag). risks section addresses PostgreSQL throughput concerns indirectly through the escalation and directly through Redis failure scenarios. MJ does not commit blindly — confidence.level is 55 with extensive low_confidence_areas acknowledging missing scale parameters. The output is thorough and well-structured.

observations.text: Excellent escalation: explicitly frames PostgreSQL vs domain rule as a conflict, not just a preference, with concrete threshold (~500 couriers) where it breaks down
Confidence level of 55 appropriately reflects missing scale information — well below the 70 threshold
Three options presented in escalation with honest assessment of each, including acknowledging PG-only works at low scale
trade_offs section is exceptionally detailed — each decision lists gains, sacrifices, AND alternatives_considered
StalenessEnforcer as a pure domain service with defense-in-depth (domain + Redis TTL) shows strong architectural thinking
Clock skew risk identified and mitigated with server-side timestamps — a subtle but important concern

While the escalation identifies missing information, it doesn't frame it as a formal blocking question requiring answers before proceeding — MJ still recommends Option B despite acknowledging scale is unknown

The output leans heavily toward Option B (Redis) despite explicitly noting that PG-only might work at low scale and that scale parameters are unknown — a more balanced stance would defer the recommendation until scale is clarified

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Domain-architecture conflict explicitly identified in escalations section — PostgreSQL-only vs Bird's 30s staleness rule. (2) Formal escalation with specific questions about team Redis expertise, fleet size, update frequency, and whether Bird's rule is absolute or has degradation modes. (3) Multiple architectural options presented (PostgreSQL-only, Redis+PostgreSQL, PostgreSQL with materialized view). (4) trade_offs includes clear sacrifices for the recommended option (additional infrastructure, operational burden). (5) Risks include PostgreSQL write throughput concerns. (6) MJ does not commit without missing info — confidence is 55, well below 70 threshold. (7) Escalation explicitly recommends confirming with Bird.

observations.text: Confidence level of 55 appropriately reflects missing context about team capabilities and scale requirements
Escalation section clearly frames the domain-architecture conflict with three concrete options and trade-offs for each
TTL-based staleness enforcement is a strong architectural insight — transforms a convention-based rule into a structural guarantee
Low confidence areas explicitly call out the unknowns: Redis expertise, exact scale, Bird's rule interpretation
Trade-offs section lists sacrifices (additional infra, operational complexity) not just gains
MJ leans heavily toward Option B (Redis) as 'recommended' despite acknowledging missing scale data — could be seen as soft-committing before getting fleet size confirmation
Risks section covers Redis failure, clock skew, write throughput, and PostgreSQL batch lag — comprehensive coverage
Implementation guidance is phased, allowing incremental adoption rather than big-bang infrastructure change

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Bird's 30-second staleness rule creates a fundamental tension with the proposed PostgreSQL-only architecture. Courier location is ephemeral, high-frequency data — writing every update to a relational database adds unnecessary latency, I/O pressure, and table bloat for data that expires in 30 seconds. The recommended architecture introduces a hot/warm split: Redis (or a similar in-memory store) serves as the real-time location layer enforcing the staleness invariant, while P
